summary: This is an advice column where Cathy O'Neil (as 'Aunt Pythia') answers reader questions with her friend 'Sister of My Sister.' Topics include whether to pursue math graduate school, gender disparities in college admissions, reflecting on meaningful advice given, and navigating a sexless marriage. The college admissions discussion is the most substantive, examining statistics showing women face lower acceptance rates at elite schools and exploring possible explanations including application behavior differences, SAT score disparities, and institutional incentives around future endowment contributions.
key_points: When uncertain about pursuing a passion like math grad school, do what you'd do if you weren't insecure - wanting to read math in spare time is a very good sign
Elite private colleges show significant gender gaps in acceptance rates favoring men, possibly constituting de facto affirmative action for male applicants
Statistical disparities in admissions could reflect differences in application behavior, but more likely reflect institutional preferences embedded in algorithms and processes

Colleges may implicitly favor male applicants because men are more likely to become major endowment contributors in a sexist society - such biases get embedded in processes rather than stated explicitly
The most rewarding advice helps people stop worrying and start feeling good now, because 'there's really no time to lose when it comes to just getting on with your life'
money_quotes: Do what you'd do if you weren't insecure.
We never know whether we have what it takes for anything. At least we who are not crazy narcissistic don't.
A cynical person would point out that what colleges really care about is future endowment contributions, and in our sexist society men are more likely to be the contributors to that.
It's more likely to be embedded in an algorithm or at least a process, as many such assumptions are.
Both 'grades' and 'SAT scores' are somewhat arbitrary systems of judgement, neither of which are particularly convincing to me of inner intrinsic worth.
There's really no time to lose when it comes to just getting on with your life.
For an unromantic atheist such as myself, marriages are not simply friendships, they are sexual arrangements.
key_insight: Institutional biases often get embedded in algorithms and processes rather than being explicitly stated, making them harder to identify and challenge even when statistical evidence suggests discrimination.
